Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me compile the findings into a clear answer.

---

## EU AI Act: Implementing Acts, Delegated Acts, and Codes of Practice Published in the Past 12 Months

Based on my research, here is what the European Commission and the AI Office have published since approximately March 2024:

---

### 1. CODES OF PRACTICE

#### A. General-Purpose AI Code of Practice (GPAI CoP) — **Published 10 July 2025; Approved 1 August 2025**

This is the most significant regulatory instrument published in the past 12 months. The Code was developed through four iterative drafts (November 2024, December 2024, March 2025, and the final version on 10 July 2025) with input from thousands of industry participants, independent experts, and civil society.

- **Legal basis:** Article 56 of the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689)
- **Structure:** Three chapters covering (i) Transparency and Copyright, (ii) Systemic Risk Taxonomy and Safety & Security Mitigations, and (iii) Systemic Risk Governance
- **Purpose:** Voluntary compliance tool for providers of general-purpose AI models to demonstrate compliance with obligations under Articles 53 and 55
- **Approval:** The Commission approved the Code on **1 August 2025**, giving it general validity within the Union via an implementing act (as confirmed by the TwoBirds guide and Jones Day analysis)
- **Deadline:** The Code was required to be ready by 2 May 2025 under Article 56(9); the final version was published shortly after, and the fallback deadline for Commission implementing acts was 2 August 2025

**Sources:**
- Jones Day, "EU AI Act: European Commission Publishes General-Purpose AI Code of Practice" (August 2025) — https://www.jonesday.com/en/insights/2025/08/eu-ai-act-european-commission-publishes-generalpurpose-ai-code-of-practice
- Nelson Mullins, "The EU Commission Publishes General-Purpose AI Code of Practice" (July 2025) — https://www.nelsonmullins.com/insights/alerts/privacy_and_data_security_alert/all/the-eu-commission-publishes-general-purpose-ai-code-of-practice-compliance-obligations-begin-august-2025
- TwoBirds, "European Union Artificial Intelligence Act: a guide" (PDF) — confirms approval on 1 August 2025

#### B. Draft Code of Practice on Transparency of AI-Generated Content — **First Draft Published 17 December 2025**

- **Legal basis:** Article 50(7) of the EU AI Act
- **Scope:** Covers watermarking, labelling, and detection of AI-generated or manipulated content (including deepfakes) under Article 50(2) and (4)
- **Status:** First draft only; final version expected by June 2026, ahead of the August 2026 application date for Article 50 obligations
- **Process:** Developed by two Working Groups established in November 2025, incorporating 187 written submissions

**Sources:**
- Jones Day, "European Commission Publishes Draft Code of Practice on AI Labelling and Transparency" (January 2026) — https://www.jonesday.com/en/insights/2026/01/european-commission-publishes-draft-code-of-practice-on-ai-labelling-and-transparency
- Cooley, "EU AI Act: First Draft Code of Practice on Transparency and Watermarking Released" (December 2025) — https://www.cooley.com/news/insight/2025/2025-12-18-eu-ai-act-first-draft-code-of-practice-on-transparency-and-watermarking-released

---

### 2. COMMISSION GUIDELINES (Non-binding, under Article 96)

While not implementing or delegated acts, these are significant regulatory instruments published in the past 12 months:

#### A. Guidelines on Prohibited AI Practices — **4 February 2025**
- Clarifies the eight prohibited AI practices under Article 5
- Available in all EU official languages as of 29 July 2025
- Non-binding; authoritative interpretation reserved for the CJEU

#### B. Guidelines on the Definition of an "AI System" — **6 February 2025**
- Clarifies the scope of what constitutes an AI system under Article 3(1)
- Published alongside the prohibited practices guidelines

#### C. Guidelines on the Scope of Obligations for GPAI Model Providers — **18 July 2025**
- Clarifies the definition and scope of GPAI models, lifecycle obligations, systemic risk criteria, and notification duties under Chapter V (Articles 51–56)

---

### 3. TRAINING DATA SUMMARY TEMPLATE — **24 July 2025**

The AI Office published the mandatory template for the "sufficiently detailed summary about the content used for training" of GPAI models, as required by Article 53(1)(d). This is a regulatory instrument with binding effect, though not formally an implementing act.

**Source:** WilmerHale, "European Commission Releases Mandatory Template for Public Disclosure of AI Training Data" (July 2025) — https://www.wilmerhale.com/en/insights/blogs/wilmerhale-privacy-and-cybersecurity-law/european-commission-releases-mandatory-template-for-public-disclosure-of-ai-training-data

---

### 4. FORMAL IMPLEMENTING AND DELEGATED ACTS

**As of the current date, the Commission does not appear to have formally adopted standalone implementing or delegated regulations (published in the Official Journal L series) under the EU AI Act.** Specifically:

- **No delegated act** has been adopted to amend Annex I (list of AI techniques/approaches, Article 4), Annex III (high-risk use cases, Article 7), or Annex XIII (systemic risk criteria, Article 51(4)).
- **No implementing act** has been adopted for common specifications under Article 41 (for high-risk AI system requirements).
- The GPAI Code of Practice was approved via an implementing act on 1 August 2025, but this appears to have been an approval decision rather than a standalone implementing regulation published in the OJ.
- The Commission's power to adopt implementing acts for common rules on GPAI obligations (Article 56(9)) was not triggered, as the Code of Practice was finalised before the 2 August 2025 fallback deadline.

---

### Summary Table

| Instrument | Date | Type | Legal Basis | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Guidelines on Prohibited AI Practices | 4 Feb 2025 | Guideline (Art. 96) | Art. 5 | Final |
| Guidelines on AI System Definition | 6 Feb 2025 | Guideline (Art. 96) | Art. 3(1) | Final |
| GPAI Code of Practice | 10 Jul 2025 (published) / 1 Aug 2025 (approved) | Code of Practice (Art. 56) | Arts. 53, 55 | **Approved via implementing act** |
| Guidelines on GPAI Obligations | 18 Jul 2025 | Guideline (Art. 96) | Arts. 51–56 | Final |
| Training Data Summary Template | 24 Jul 2025 | Template (Art. 53(1)(d)) | Art. 53(1)(d) | Final / Mandatory |
| Draft Code of Practice on Transparency of AI-Generated Content | 17 Dec 2025 | Code of Practice (Art. 50(7)) | Art. 50 | First Draft |

**Key takeaway:** The most significant formal instrument in the past 12 months is the **GPAI Code of Practice**, approved by the Commission via implementing act on 1 August 2025. No standalone delegated regulations or implementing regulations have yet been published in the Official Journal. The Commission's guideline activity has been robust, with three major guideline packages published in February and July 2025.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer correctly identifies the GPAI Code of Practice as the key instrument, provides publication/approval dates, legal basis, and content, and cites relevant sources, fully meeting the reference requirements.

output.answer:
I now have a comprehensive picture. Let me compile the full answer.

---

## Most Recent FDA Guidance on AI/ML-Enabled Medical Devices

### FDA's Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP) Final Guidance — December 4, 2024

The most significant recent FDA action is the **final guidance issued on December 4, 2024**: *"Marketing Submission Recommendations for a Predetermined Change Control Plan for Artificial Intelligence-Enabled Device Software Functions."* This is the centerpiece of FDA's current approach to AI/ML devices.

**Key provisions:**

- **Predetermined Change Control Plans (PCCPs):** The guidance allows manufacturers to pre-specify planned modifications to AI-enabled device software functions and submit them as part of the original marketing authorization (510(k), De Novo, or PMA). This means iterative algorithm updates can be implemented without requiring new submissions for each change — provided they fall within the pre-authorized PCCP boundaries.

- **What a PCCP must include:** A description of planned modifications, the methodology for implementing and validating those changes, and an assessment of how the changes will continue to ensure safety and effectiveness.

- **Total Product Lifecycle (TPLC) Approach:** The PCCP guidance operationalizes FDA's TPLC philosophy, which recognizes that AI/ML devices evolve post-market and need oversight across their entire lifecycle — not just at the pre-market stage.

**Supporting framework — Good Machine Learning Practice (GMLP):** The PCCP guidance builds on the **October 2021 GMLP Guiding Principles** (jointly issued with Health Canada and the UK MHRA), which established ten principles including: rigorous software engineering, representative training datasets, human-AI team performance evaluation, and post-deployment monitoring. Additional GMLP-aligned guidance includes:

- **October 2023:** *Predetermined Change Control Plans for Machine Learning-Enabled Medical Devices: Guiding Principles*
- **June 2024:** *Transparency for Machine Learning-Enabled Medical Devices: Guiding Principles*

**Scale of FDA AI/ML authorizations:** As of December 20, 2024, the FDA had authorized **1,016 AI/ML-enabled medical devices**, spanning radiology, cardiology, pathology, and other specialties.

---

## How the FDA Framework Interacts with the EU AI Act's High-Risk Classification

### The EU AI Act's Classification of Medical AI as High-Risk

Under the EU AI Act, AI-enabled medical devices are classified as **high-risk** through two complementary routes:

**Route 1 — Article 6(1) (Product Safety Route):** An AI system is high-risk when:
- (a) It is a **safety component of a product, or is itself a product**, covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in **Annex II**; **and**
- (b) The product must undergo **third-party conformity assessment** under that legislation.

Recital 30 explicitly names **medical devices** and **in vitro diagnostic medical devices** as products falling within this scope. The relevant legislation is **Regulation (EU) 2017/745 (MDR)** and **Regulation (EU) 2017/746 (IVDR)**. Since most medium- and high-risk medical devices under the MDR require notified body (third-party) conformity assessment, AI systems that are themselves medical devices — or are safety components of medical devices — are automatically classified as high-risk under the AI Act.

**Route 2 — Annex III (Standalone Route):** Even if an AI system is not a medical device per se, it could still be classified as high-risk if it falls within the areas listed in Annex III (e.g., AI used for triage, diagnosis, or treatment decisions in healthcare).

### EU AI Act Requirements for High-Risk Medical AI

Once classified as high-risk, the AI system must comply with **Chapter 2 of Title III**, which includes:

| Requirement | Article | Summary |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Management System** | Art. 9 | Continuous iterative process throughout the entire lifecycle; identify, evaluate, and mitigate known and foreseeable risks |
| **Data & Data Governance** | Art. 10 | Training/validation/testing datasets must be relevant, representative, free from errors, and complete; data governance practices must be documented |
| **Technical Documentation** | Art. 11 | Comprehensive documentation demonstrating compliance |
| **Record-Keeping** | Art. 12 | Automatic logging of events during operation |
| **Transparency & Information** | Art. 13 | Users must be able to interpret output and use it appropriately |
| **Human Oversight** | Art. 14 | Built-in measures enabling natural persons to oversee the system, detect anomalies, and override or disregard outputs |
| **Accuracy, Robustness & Cybersecurity** | Art. 15 | Appropriate accuracy levels declared; resilience to errors, faults, and adversarial attacks; feedback loops addressed |
| **Quality Management System** | Art. 17 | Documented QMS covering design, development, verification, data management, and modification management |
| **Conformity Assessment** | Art. 43 | Third-party notified body assessment integrated with MDR/IVDR procedures |

### Conformity Assessment Integration

Critically, **Article 43(3)** provides that for AI systems covered by Annex II legislation (including the MDR/IVDR), the conformity assessment of the AI Act requirements is integrated into the **existing notified body assessment** under those regulations. The MDR/IVDR notified body assesses compliance with both the medical device regulation and the AI Act requirements in a single procedure. This avoids duplicative assessments.

Additionally, **Article 48(6)** provides that for medical devices, the derogation procedures under Article 59 of the MDR and Article 54 of the IVDR apply to the AI Act conformity assessment as well.

### Key Points of Interaction and Tension

**1. Change Management — PCCP vs. "Substantial Modification":**

This is the most significant interaction point. The FDA's PCCP guidance allows pre-authorized iterative changes without new submissions. Under the EU AI Act, **Article 43(4)** requires a **new conformity assessment whenever a high-risk AI system is "substantially modified."** The AI Act does not yet have a PCCP-equivalent mechanism. A manufacturer with an FDA PCCP may still need to go through a new EU notified body assessment for changes that the AI Act considers "substantial" — even if the FDA would not require a new submission. This creates a potential regulatory asymmetry.

**2. Continuous Learning Systems:**

The EU AI Act explicitly addresses systems that "continue to learn after being placed on the market" in **Article 15(3)**, requiring that feedback loops and biased outputs be "duly addressed with appropriate mitigation measures." The FDA's TPLC approach and PCCP framework similarly acknowledge continuous learning, but the regulatory mechanisms differ: FDA uses pre-authorized change boundaries, while the EU relies on post-market monitoring (Article 72) and re-assessment triggers.

**3. Human Oversight:**

The EU AI Act's **Article 14** mandates specific human oversight measures — including the ability to "override or disregard" AI outputs. The FDA's GMLP principles address human-AI team performance but do not mandate a specific "human-in-the-loop" override mechanism in the same prescriptive way. This is a potential gap for manufacturers seeking dual compliance.

**4. Transparency:**

The FDA's June 2024 transparency guiding principles and the EU AI Act's **Article 13** both emphasize transparency, but the EU Act is more prescriptive about what information must be provided to users (including the system's intended purpose, accuracy levels, and limitations).

**5. Cybersecurity:**

The EU AI Act's **Article 15(4)** requires resilience against unauthorized third-party attempts to alter system performance. Article 42(2) creates a presumption of conformity if the system is certified under the EU Cybersecurity Act (Regulation 2019/881). The FDA's cybersecurity expectations for medical devices (under its 2014/2023 premarket cybersecurity guidance) are broadly aligned but operate under a different legal framework.

### Summary Table: FDA vs. EU AI Act for Medical AI

| Dimension | FDA (PCCP + GMLP) | EU AI Act (High-Risk) |
|---|---|---|
| Classification trigger | Device intended for medical purpose (FD&C Act) | Safety component/product under MDR/IVDR requiring third-party assessment |
| Pre-market pathway | 510(k), De Novo, PMA with optional PCCP | MDR/IVDR notified body assessment + AI Act Chapter 2 compliance |
| Iterative changes | Pre-authorized via PCCP | New conformity assessment if "substantially modified" |
| Risk management | QSR/ISO 14971 aligned | Art. 9 — continuous iterative lifecycle process |
| Human oversight | GMLP principle (human-AI team) | Art. 14 — mandatory, with override capability |
| Post-market | TPLC monitoring | Art. 72 post-market monitoring + Art. 26 deployer obligations |
| Transparency | June 2024 guiding principles | Art. 13 — mandatory information provision |

### Practical Takeaway

A manufacturer developing an AI/ML medical device for both the US and EU markets must navigate two converging but not identical frameworks. The FDA's PCCP guidance offers a more flexible change-management pathway, while the EU AI Act imposes more prescriptive requirements around human oversight, transparency, and re-assessment triggers. The most efficient compliance strategy is to design the quality management system (Art. 17) and risk management system (Art. 9) to satisfy both regimes simultaneously, using the more stringent EU requirements as the baseline and layering the FDA PCCP documentation on top. Early engagement with both an FDA review division and an EU notified body is strongly advisable to align on what constitutes a "substantial modification" under the AI Act versus a PCCP-covered change under FDA guidance.

scores.judge_rationale: The answer correctly identifies the December 2024 PCCP final guidance as the most recent FDA action and thoroughly explains its interaction with the EU AI Act's high-risk classification, citing both the FDA guidance and EU AI Act Annex I/III provisions.

I now have a comprehensive picture of both the SOP and the HIPAA BAA requirements. Let me compile the gap analysis.

---

## Gap Analysis: SOP-AIML-002 vs. HIPAA Business Associate Agreement Requirements

### Overview

SOP-AIML-002 contains only a single, brief paragraph addressing Business Associate Agreements (Section 6.5), which reads:

> *"Where training data is sourced from or involves HIPAA-covered entities, a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) shall be in place prior to data receipt. The Legal department maintains BAA records. The AI/ML Lead shall verify BAA coverage exists before initiating data transfer from a covered entity source. Any data sharing with vendors or partners involving PHI requires a downstream BAA executed by the Legal department."*

This is a high-level acknowledgment, but it falls far short of operationalizing the full set of HIPAA BAA obligations under **45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)** and **§ 164.314(a)(2)**. Below are the specific missing elements.

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 1: Permitted Uses and Disclosures of PHI (45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)(i))

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must specify the permitted uses and disclosures of PHI by the business associate. Under § 164.502(a)(3), a business associate may use or disclose PHI *only* as permitted by its BAA or as required by law.

**What's missing from the SOP:** The SOP never addresses how training data containing PHI may be used once received. There is no requirement that the BAA explicitly enumerate permitted purposes (e.g., "model training for clinical decision support," "bias assessment," "data quality evaluation"). Without this, Meridian risks using PHI for purposes beyond what the covered entity authorized — a direct violation of § 164.504(e)(2)(i)(A) and § 164.502(a)(3).

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 2: Prohibition on Further Use or Disclosure (45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(A))

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must require the business associate to *not use or further disclose PHI other than as permitted or required by the contract or as required by law.*

**What's missing from the SOP:** The SOP is silent on restricting internal use of PHI-containing training data to only the purposes enumerated in the BAA. For example, nothing prevents a data scientist from repurposing PHI-containing clinical data originally sourced for a diagnostic imaging model to train an unrelated patient risk-scoring model — unless the BAA explicitly constrains this. The SOP should require that each dataset's Data Card (Section 5.8) explicitly reference the BAA-permitted use scope and prohibit uses outside it.

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 3: Appropriate Safeguards and Security Rule Compliance (45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(B))

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must require the business associate to *use appropriate safeguards and comply, where applicable, with Subpart C (the Security Rule) with respect to ePHI.*

**What's missing from the SOP:** While Section 6 (Controls and Safeguards) addresses access controls, encryption, and audit logging, the SOP never explicitly ties these controls to the HIPAA Security Rule obligations that flow through the BAA. Specifically missing:

- **No requirement that BAA-covered training datasets be explicitly tagged** as subject to HIPAA Security Rule compliance (e.g., administrative, physical, and technical safeguards per § 164.306).
- **No requirement for a HIPAA-specific risk assessment** (§ 164.308(a)(1)(ii)(A)) for training data environments handling ePHI.
- **No reference to the Security Rule's specific implementation specifications** (e.g., § 164.312 access controls, audit controls, integrity controls, person/entity authentication, transmission security).

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 4: Breach Notification and Security Incident Reporting (45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(C); § 164.314(a)(2)(i)(C); § 164.410)

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must require the business associate to *report to the covered entity any use or disclosure of PHI not provided for by the contract, including breaches of unsecured PHI as required by § 164.410.* Under § 164.410, notification must occur *without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery.*

**What's missing from the SOP:** The SOP contains no operational procedure for:

- **Identifying when a training data incident constitutes a HIPAA breach** (improper access, use, or disclosure of PHI in training data).
- **Timely notification to the covered entity** — the 60-day clock under § 164.410(b) is not referenced anywhere.
- **Content of breach notification** — § 164.410(c) requires identification of each affected individual and other information the covered entity needs for its own notification obligations under § 164.404(c). The SOP is silent on this.
- **Coordination between the CISO (incident response) and the CCO/Legal** for BAA breach notification obligations. Section 8.4 (Emergency Procedures) references SOP-RM-001 for incident management but never mentions BAA breach notification obligations.

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 5: Subcontractor / Downstream BAA Requirements (45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(D); § 164.314(a)(2)(i)(B))

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must require the business associate to *ensure that any subcontractors that create, receive, maintain, or transmit PHI agree to the same restrictions and conditions that apply to the business associate.* Under § 164.314(a)(2)(i)(B), the business associate must ensure subcontractors enter into agreements that comply with § 164.314.

**What's missing from the SOP:** Section 6.5 mentions that *"any data sharing with vendors or partners involving PHI requires a downstream BAA executed by the Legal department"* — but this is critically incomplete:

- **No requirement for cascading BAAs to labeling vendors** (Section 5.4.2 covers external annotators but only requires "confidentiality agreements" — not BAAs).
- **No requirement for BAAs with cloud infrastructure providers** that may process ePHI (AWS, Azure are mentioned in Section 1.2).
- **No requirement for BAAs with synthetic data generation vendors** or third-party data source providers who may touch PHI.
- **No verification procedure** that downstream BAAs contain the same substantive obligations (permitted uses, safeguards, breach reporting, return/destruction, termination, HHS access).

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 6: Individual Rights — Access, Amendment, and Accounting (45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(E), (F), (G))

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must require the business associate to:
- **(E)** Make PHI available for individual access under § 164.524;
- **(F)** Make PHI available for amendment and incorporate amendments under § 164.526;
- **(G)** Make available information needed for an accounting of disclosures under § 164.528.

**What's missing from the SOP:** The SOP is entirely silent on these obligations. Training data containing PHI may be subject to individual access requests (e.g., a patient requesting to see what data Meridian holds about them for AI training). The SOP references SOP-PRIV-003 (Data Subject Access Request Handling) in Section 10.1, but:

- **No procedure for locating an individual's PHI within training datasets** — training data is often aggregated, transformed, and embedded in model parameters, making individual record retrieval non-trivial.
- **No procedure for amending PHI in training data** if a covered entity notifies Meridian of an amendment obligation.
- **No procedure for generating an accounting of disclosures** from training data repositories.

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 7: HHS Access to Internal Practices, Books, and Records (45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(I))

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must require the business associate to *make its internal practices, books, and records relating to the use and disclosure of PHI available to the Secretary of HHS for compliance determination purposes.*

**What's missing from the SOP:** The SOP does not address HHS inspection or audit readiness specific to training data containing PHI. While Section 5.8.3 mentions Data Cards being "exportable in machine-readable format for audit and regulatory purposes," there is no:

- **Procedure for responding to an HHS request** for records related to PHI use in training data.
- **Designation of who handles HHS audit requests** (the RACI matrix in Section 3.1 assigns "BAA Validation" to CISO (R) and CPO/DPO (A) and CCO (R), but this is limited to validation, not ongoing compliance or HHS response).

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 8: PHI Return or Destruction at Termination (45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(J))

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must require that *at termination of the contract, if feasible, return or destroy all PHI received from or created by the business associate on behalf of the covered entity, and retain no copies — or, if return/destruction is not feasible, extend protections and limit further uses.*

**What's missing from the SOP:** This is a critical gap for AI/ML training data specifically:

- **No procedure for destroying PHI embedded in trained models** — once a model is trained on PHI-containing data, the model weights may encode information derived from PHI. The SOP's data lifecycle (Section 5.6.2) includes "Archived" state but does not address BAA-termination-triggered destruction.
- **No procedure for identifying all copies of PHI** across development sandboxes, staging environments, backup systems, and model artifacts.
- **No "inf feasibility" determination process** — for AI/ML, it may genuinely be infeasible to "untrain" a model. The SOP should require a documented infeasibility determination with compensating controls (extending protections, limiting further uses) as required by § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(J).
- **Section 5.1.3** mentions "explicit terms for data deletion upon contract termination" for third-party data, but this is framed as a contractual provision for commercial data, not as a HIPAA BAA obligation.

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 9: Termination Authorization for Material Breach (45 CFR § 164.504(e)(2)(iii))

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must *authorize termination of the contract by the covered entity if the covered entity determines the business associate has violated a material term of the contract.*

**What's missing from the SOP:** The SOP does not address what happens to training data and models when a BAA is terminated due to Meridian's breach. There is no:

- **Procedure for covered entity termination notification** flowing to the AI/ML team.
- **Process for identifying all models and datasets** dependent on PHI from the terminating covered entity.
- **Contingency plan** for model decommissioning or retraining without the affected PHI.

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 10: Minimum Necessary Standard (45 CFR § 164.502(b); § 164.514(d))

**What HIPAA requires:** When using or disclosing PHI, a covered entity or business associate must make *reasonable efforts to limit PHI to the minimum necessary to accomplish the intended purpose.*

**What's missing from the SOP:** Section 4.6 (Data Minimization) addresses the concept generally but does not operationalize it for HIPAA compliance:

- **No requirement that each PHI data element in a training dataset be justified against the BAA-permitted purpose.**
- **No HIPAA-specific minimum necessary review** at the data sourcing stage (Section 5.1.1). The sourcing request includes "Data elements requested with justification for each" but does not require a specific HIPAA minimum necessary determination.
- **No procedure for de-identification** per § 164.514(a)-(c) (Safe Harbor or Expert Determination methods) as an alternative to using PHI directly. The SOP mentions "de-identified" data in access tiers but never requires de-identification as a HIPAA compliance strategy before training.

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 11: BAA Operational Integration into the Data Sourcing Workflow

**What HIPAA requires:** The BAA must be in place *before* PHI is disclosed (§ 164.504(e)(1)).

**What's missing from the SOP:** Section 5.1.1 (Sourcing Evaluation Process) does not include a BAA verification gate. The five-step sourcing process (Request → Preliminary Vetting → Sample Evaluation → Full Approval → Acquisition) never explicitly checks for BAA existence. Section 6.5 says the "AI/ML Lead shall verify BAA coverage exists before initiating data transfer," but this is:

- **Not embedded as a mandatory gate** in the sourcing workflow.
- **Not tracked in the Dataset Registry** — there is no field in the Data Card (Section 5.8) for BAA reference or PHI status.
- **Not subject to automated enforcement** — the system does not block data ingestion if BAA verification hasn't occurred.

---

### MISSING ELEMENT 12: BAA Coverage for Internal PHI Data Sources

Section 5.1.2 addresses internal data sources and requires "verification of HIPAA compliance pathway (treatment, operations, or authorized research)." However, when Meridian itself is a business associate of a covered entity (e.g., a hospital system providing EHR data), the SOP does not distinguish between:

- PHI received under a BAA where Meridian is the **business associate** (subject to all § 164.504(e)(2) obligations).
- PHI within Meridian's own systems where Meridian may be a **covered entity** in its own right (e.g., employee health data).

The compliance obligations differ materially, and the SOP conflates these scenarios.

---

### Summary Table of Missing Elements

| # | Missing Element | HIPAA Citation | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Permitted uses enumeration for PHI in training data | § 164.504(e)(2)(i); § 164.502(a)(3) | **Critical** |
| 2 | Prohibition on further use/disclosure beyond BAA scope | § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(A) | **Critical** |
| 3 | HIPAA Security Rule compliance tagging for BAA-covered datasets | § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(B); Subpart C | **Critical** |
| 4 | Breach notification procedure (60-day clock, content, covered entity coordination) | § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(C); § 164.410 | **Critical** |
| 5 | Cascading BAA requirements for labeling vendors, cloud providers, data vendors | § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(D); § 164.314(a)(2)(i)(B) | **Critical** |
| 6 | Individual rights procedures (access, amendment, accounting) for PHI in training data | § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(E)-(G) | **Major** |
| 7 | HHS audit/inspection readiness for training data records | § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(I) | **Major** |
| 8 | PHI return/destruction at BAA termination (including model weights) | § 164.504(e)(2)(ii)(J) | **Critical** |
| 9 | Covered entity termination rights and operational response | § 164.504(e)(2)(iii) | **Major** |
| 10 | HIPAA minimum necessary operationalization for training data | § 164.502(b); § 164.514(d) | **Major** |
| 11 | BAA verification as a mandatory, automated gate in sourcing workflow | § 164.504(e)(1) | **Major** |
| 12 | Distinction between Meridian as BA vs. covered entity for internal PHI | § 164.504(e) generally | **Major** |

---

### Conclusion

SOP-AIML-002's Section 6.5 is a single, high-level paragraph that acknowledges the existence of BAAs but fails entirely to operationalize the twelve categories of HIPAA BAA obligations identified above. The SOP treats BAA compliance as a purely legal/contractual checkbox ("Legal department maintains BAA records") rather than integrating BAA obligations into the training data management lifecycle. For an organization handling PHI-containing clinical data for AI/ML model training across multiple product lines (Clinical AI Platform, MedInsight Analytics), these gaps represent significant regulatory exposure.

**Compliance level: GAP**

**Recommended priority:** Remediate Critical items (1–5, 8) before the next BAA-covered data ingestion; address Major items (6–7, 9–12) within the current SOP review cycle.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer thoroughly identifies all four missing elements from the reference and provides extensive additional detail, but it cites broader HIPAA sections (§164.504(e)(2), §164.314(a)(2)) rather than the specific expected citations (§164.502(e), §164.504(e), §164.314(a), §164.312(b)).

question.question: Reviewing SOP-FIN-001 (Credit Scoring Model Governance) against NIST AI RMF's GOVERN and MAP functions, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) full operationalisation of GOVERN — named AI governance body, escalation lines, accountability matrix, training requirements for risk owners; (2) complete AI system inventory under MAP 1 — not all credit-scoring AI systems are catalogued with risk profiles, intended use, deployment context, and stakeholder list; (3) MAP 2 stakeholder engagement procedures — affected-borrower groups and consumer advocacy input on bias and fair-lending impacts; (4) explicit linkage between MAP-identified risks and MEASURE metrics for credit-scoring fairness.
question.notes: Gap detection across GOVERN+MAP for a partial SOP.

output.answer:
I now have sufficient information from both the SOP and the NIST AI RMF framework to conduct a thorough gap analysis. Let me compile the findings.

---

# Gap Analysis: SOP-FIN-001 vs. NIST AI RMF 1.0 — GOVERN & MAP Functions

## Methodology

I reviewed the full text of SOP-FIN-001 (v3.6, dated 2025-09-03) against every category and subcategory of the NIST AI RMF 1.0 GOVERN and MAP functions (NIST AI 100-1, January 26, 2023). The SOP explicitly claims alignment with NIST AI RMF 1.0 in Section 1.3 and references "AI risk categories (GOVERN, MAP)" in Revision History v3.0. Below are the specific elements that are **missing, under-addressed, or absent** from the SOP.

---

## GOVERN FUNCTION — MISSING ELEMENTS

### GOVERN 1.2: Characteristics of Trustworthy AI Integrated into Policies
**What's Missing:** The SOP does not systematically integrate the seven characteristics of trustworthy AI (valid & reliable, safe, secure & resilient, accountable & transparent, explainable & interpretable, privacy-enhanced, fair with harmful bias managed) as an explicit framework. While fairness (4.2), explainability (4.3), privacy (6.1), and security (6.2) are addressed individually, there is no unified policy statement mapping each trustworthiness characteristic to specific controls. The SOP lacks a holistic "trustworthy AI" policy integration — it treats these as separate compliance obligations rather than as an integrated characteristics framework.

### GOVERN 1.3: Risk Tolerance Determination
**What's Missing:** The SOP defines risk tiers (A/B/C) based on materiality and complexity (Section 4.4), but it does **not** document the organization's overarching **risk tolerance** or **risk appetite** statement. NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.3 requires that "processes, procedures, and practices are in place to determine the needed level of risk management activities based on the organization's risk tolerance." The SOP references "the Board's risk appetite" in Section 1.1 but never defines, documents, or operationalizes what that risk appetite actually is for AI systems. There is no explicit risk tolerance threshold that governs when a model is deemed too risky to deploy.

### GOVERN 1.4: Transparent Risk Management Process Based on Organizational Risk Priorities
**What's Missing:** While the SOP has detailed procedures, there is no documented mechanism that transparently communicates the risk management process and its outcomes to stakeholders beyond the immediate governance chain. The SOP lacks provisions for transparency about *why* certain risk priorities were chosen over others, and how risk management outcomes are shared across the organization.

### GOVERN 1.5: Ongoing Monitoring and Periodic Review of the Risk Management *Process Itself*
**What's Missing:** The SOP has excellent ongoing monitoring of *models* (Section 7) and annual re-validation of Tier A models (Section 7.3). However, it does **not** include a planned periodic review of the **risk management process itself** — i.e., a meta-review of whether the governance framework is working effectively. NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.5 explicitly requires "ongoing monitoring and periodic review of the risk management process and its outcomes" with defined frequency. The SOP reviews models, not the governance process.

### GOVERN 1.6: Mechanisms to Inventory AI Systems Resourced According to Risk Priorities
**What's Missing:** The MRMS inventory (Section 5.2) captures models, but the SOP does not address whether the inventory system itself is **resourced according to organizational risk priorities**. There is no provision ensuring that higher-risk models receive proportionally more resources for inventory management, documentation, or oversight staffing.

### GOVERN 3.1: Diverse Team Informing Decision-Making
**What's Missing — Critical Gap:** NIST AI RMF GOVERN 3.1 requires that "decision-making related to mapping, measuring, and managing AI risks throughout the lifecycle is informed by a diverse team (e.g., diversity of demographics, disciplines, experience, expertise, and backgrounds)." The SOP's governance structure (Section 3) is composed entirely of senior executives and technical validators. There is **no requirement for multidisciplinary diversity** — no inclusion of legal, ethics, consumer advocacy, behavioral science, or community representation perspectives in governance decisions. The RACI matrix is exclusively internal corporate roles. There is no mechanism to ensure demographic, disciplinary, or experiential diversity in decision-making bodies.

### GOVERN 3.2: Policies Defining Human-AI Configurations and Oversight
**What's Missing — Partial:** Section 4.5 addresses human oversight for Tier A models per the EU AI Act, but the SOP does not have policies that broadly "define and differentiate roles and responsibilities for human-AI configurations" across all risk tiers. For Tier B and C models, there is no documented human-AI configuration policy. The SOP also does not address how human-AI teaming should work beyond the override mechanism.

### GOVERN 4.1: Critical Thinking and Safety-First Mindset
**What's Missing:** The SOP does not include organizational policies or practices to "foster a critical thinking and safety-first mindset in the design, development, deployment, and uses of AI systems." The training curriculum (Section 9) covers compliance and technical topics but does not include modules on critical thinking, challenging assumptions, or safety-first culture.

### GOVERN 4.2: Documenting and Communicating Risks and Impacts Broadly
**What's Missing:** The SOP requires documentation of model risks (Model Narrative, Validation Reports), but does not require that teams "communicate about the impacts more broadly" across the organization. There is no mechanism for sharing lessons learned, risk insights, or near-miss incidents across model teams or business units.

### GOVERN 4.3: Organizational Practices for AI Testing, Identification of Incidents, and Information Sharing
**What's Missing:** The SOP lacks organizational practices for systematic identification and tracking of AI incidents, errors, and near-misses beyond the KPI alert system. There is no incident database, no lessons-learned repository, and no mechanism for sharing incident information across teams to prevent recurrence.

### GOVERN 5 (Entire Category): Policies, Processes, Procedures, and Practices Are in Place, Transparent, and Implemented Effectively
**What's Missing:** The NIST AI RMF 1.0 GOVERN 5 category (and its subcategories 5.1–5.7) addresses transparency, documentation, and accountability of policies and procedures. The SOP does not address:
- **GOVERN 5.1:** Transparency of AI risk management policies to external stakeholders
- **GOVERN 5.2:** Mechanisms for external parties to access information about AI risk management
- **GOVERN 5.3:** Public disclosure of AI system use and associated risks
- **GOVERN 5.4–5.7:** Broader organizational transparency and accountability mechanisms

### GOVERN 6 (Entire Category): Diverse Perspectives and Multidisciplinary Stakeholder Engagement
**What's Missing — Critical Gap:** The NIST AI RMF GOVERN 6 category requires that diverse and multidisciplinary perspectives are incorporated throughout the AI risk management process, including engagement with external stakeholders. The SOP has **no provisions for external stakeholder engagement** — no consumer advisory panels, no community impact assessments, no patient/consumer representation, no mechanism for affected communities to provide input on credit scoring models. This is a fundamental gap given that the models directly impact patients seeking healthcare financing.

---

## MAP FUNCTION — MISSING ELEMENTS

### MAP 1.1: Intended Purposes, Beneficial Uses, Context-Specific Laws, Norms, and Prospective Settings
**What's Missing — Partial:** The SOP's Model Narrative (Form M-200) requires "Executive Summary & Intended Use" (Section 5.3), which partially addresses intended purpose. However, the SOP does **not** require documentation of:
- **Potentially beneficial uses** beyond the stated business purpose
- **Context-specific norms and expectations** (e.g., patient expectations around healthcare financing, community norms)
- **Prospective deployment settings** in sufficient socio-technical detail (beyond technical non-functional requirements)

### MAP 1.2: Interdisciplinary Stakeholder Involvement
**What's Missing — Critical Gap:** NIST AI RMF MAP 1.2 requires that "the perspectives of interdisciplinary stakeholders are integrated into the mapping of AI risks." The SOP's Phase I (Initiation & Scoping) involves only the Model Owner, VP of Financial Services, and Chief AI Officer. There is **no requirement** to involve interdisciplinary perspectives (e.g., clinicians, patient advocates, social workers, ethics specialists, behavioral scientists) in establishing context for credit scoring models used in a healthcare setting.

### MAP 1.3: Organization's Risk Tolerance Incorporated into Context
**What's Missing:** As noted under GOVERN 1.3, the organization's risk tolerance is not formally documented. Consequently, MAP 1.3's requirement to incorporate risk tolerance into context establishment cannot be fulfilled.

### MAP 1.4: Assumptions About Context of Use Are Examined and Documented
**What's Missing:** The SOP does not require explicit examination and documentation of assumptions about the context of use. For example, assumptions about patient financial literacy, language barriers, health-related financial distress, or cultural attitudes toward debt are not examined.

### MAP 1.5: Potential Impacts Beyond Intended Use Are Anticipated
**What's Missing:** The SOP does not require anticipation of risks from use of AI systems **beyond intended use**. For example, credit scores being used for non-lending purposes, or model outputs influencing clinical care decisions, are not addressed.

### MAP 1.6: Practices for Ongoing Context Monitoring
**What's Missing:** The SOP's ongoing monitoring (Section 7.1) focuses on technical performance metrics (PSI, drift, default rates). It does **not** include practices for monitoring changes in the broader context — e.g., changes in patient population demographics, economic conditions, healthcare policy, or societal expectations that could alter the risk profile.

### MAP 2.2: Knowledge Limits and How Output May Be Utilized and Overseen
**What's Missing — Partial:** The Model Narrative requires documentation of "known failure modes" (Section 5.3, M-200 Section 5), but the SOP does not require comprehensive documentation of the AI system's **knowledge limits** — what the model fundamentally cannot know or predict. There is no requirement to document how system output may be utilized or overseen by humans beyond the Tier A override mechanism.

### MAP 2.3: Scientific Integrity and TEVV Considerations
**What's Missing:** The SOP does not explicitly require identification and documentation of scientific integrity considerations, including experimental design, data collection and selection rationale (beyond data quality), system trustworthiness validation, and construct validation. While validation covers conceptual soundness, the broader TEVV (Test, Evaluation, Validation, Verification) framework is not systematically applied.

### MAP 3.1: Potential Benefits Examined and Documented
**What's Missing:** The SOP focuses almost exclusively on risk mitigation. There is **no requirement** to examine and document the potential **benefits** of intended AI system functionality and performance. The business case (Phase I) may implicitly address benefits, but there is no structured benefits analysis requirement.

### MAP 3.2: Potential Costs Including Non-Monetary Costs
**What's Missing:** The SOP does not require examination of potential costs — particularly **non-monetary costs** — resulting from AI errors or system functionality issues. Non-monetary costs could include psychological harm from incorrect denials, erosion of patient trust, reputational damage, or community health impacts.

### MAP 3.4: Operator and Practitioner Proficiency
**What's Missing — Critical Gap:** NIST AI RMF MAP 3.4 requires that "processes for operator and practitioner proficiency with AI system performance and trustworthiness — and relevant technical standards and certifications — are defined, assessed, and documented." The SOP's training (Section 9) covers governance and fairness but does **not** address:
- Proficiency with AI system performance assessment
- Understanding of trustworthiness characteristics
- Relevant technical standards and certifications for operators and practitioners
- Assessment of proficiency levels

### MAP 4.1: Mapping AI Technology and Legal Risks Including Third-Party IP
**What's Missing:** The SOP addresses third-party vendor models in scope (Section 1.2) but does **not** include approaches for mapping risks related to third-party intellectual property infringement, open-source license compliance, or third-party data rights. This is a gap for models derived from open-source or vendor-supplied components.

### MAP 5.1: Likelihood and Magnitude of Impacts Based on Multiple Sources
**What's Missing — Critical Gap:** NIST AI RMF MAP 5.1 requires identification and documentation of likelihood and magnitude of each identified impact based on:
- Expected use
- **Past uses of AI systems in similar contexts** — not addressed
- **Public incident reports** — not addressed
- **Feedback from those external to the team** — not addressed
- **Other data** — not addressed

The SOP's impact assessment is internally focused and does not incorporate external incident data, industry benchmarks, or feedback from external stakeholders.

### MAP 5.2: Practices for Regular Engagement with Relevant AI Actors and Integrating Feedback
**What's Missing — Critical Gap:** The SOP has **no provisions** for regular engagement with relevant AI actors external to the organization, and no mechanism for integrating feedback about positive, negative, and unanticipated impacts. There is no consumer complaint feedback loop tied to model governance, no patient/consumer advisory mechanism, and no external stakeholder engagement process.

---

## SUMMARY OF CRITICAL MISSING ELEMENTS

| # | Missing Element | NIST AI RMF Reference | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **No external stakeholder engagement mechanisms** — no patient/consumer input, no community impact assessment, no feedback integration from affected populations | GOVERN 6.x, MAP 1.2, MAP 5.1, MAP 5.2 | **High** |
| 2 | **No documented organizational risk tolerance/appetite statement** for AI systems | GOVERN 1.3, MAP 1.3 | **High** |
| 3 | **No workforce diversity, equity, inclusion requirements** for governance decision-making bodies | GOVERN 3.1 | **High** |
| 4 | **No periodic review of the risk management process itself** (only model reviews) | GOVERN 1.5 | **Medium** |
| 5 | **No systematic integration of all seven trustworthy AI characteristics** as a unified framework | GOVERN 1.2 | **Medium** |
| 6 | **No operator/practitioner proficiency assessment** for AI system performance and trustworthiness | MAP 3.4 | **Medium** |
| 7 | **No examination of non-monetary costs** from AI errors | MAP 3.2 | **Medium** |
| 8 | **No anticipation of risks from use beyond intended purpose** | MAP 1.5 | **Medium** |
| 9 | **No organizational practices for AI incident tracking and cross-team learning** | GOVERN 4.3 | **Medium** |
| 10 | **No critical thinking / safety-first culture policies** | GOVERN 4.1 | **Medium** |
| 11 | **No third-party IP/data rights risk mapping** | MAP 4.1 | **Low** |
| 12 | **No structured benefits analysis requirement** | MAP 3.1 | **Low** |
| 13 | **No ongoing context monitoring beyond technical drift** | MAP 1.6 | **Medium** |
| 14 | **No explicit documentation of knowledge limits** for AI systems | MAP 2.2 | **Medium** |
| 15 | **No transparency mechanisms for external stakeholders** | GOVERN 5.x | **Medium** |

---

**Compliance level: Partial**

The SOP demonstrates strong alignment with SR 11-7 and the EU AI Act, and has meaningful coverage of several NIST AI RMF subcategories (particularly around model validation, bias testing, and human oversight). However, the most significant gaps cluster around: (1) **external stakeholder engagement**, (2) **organizational risk tolerance formalization**, (3) **workforce diversity in governance**, and (4) **socio-technical context mapping beyond regulatory compliance**. These are foundational GOVERN and MAP outcomes that the SOP currently does not address.

## Gap Analysis: SOP-CLIN-001 vs. EU AI Act Article 11 (Technical Documentation) and Article 17 (Quality Management System)

### Overview

SOP-CLIN-001 is a well-structured validation SOP that demonstrates awareness of the EU AI Act and incorporates several important elements. However, when measured against the specific requirements of **Article 11** (read with **Annex IV**) and **Article 17**, there are material gaps — some structural, some substantive. Below is the detailed analysis.

---

## PART 1: GAPS AGAINST ARTICLE 11 / ANNEX IV — TECHNICAL DOCUMENTATION

Annex IV of the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) specifies nine mandatory sections for technical documentation. The SOP references "Annex IV documentation elements" in Section 5.5 and states that the Final Validation Report (FVR) "includes all Annex IV documentation elements for EU AI Act compliance." However, the SOP does not enumerate, structure, or operationalize those elements. The following Annex IV items are **missing or insufficiently addressed**:

### 1. General Description of the AI System (Annex IV, §1)
- **What's required:** A general description including intended purpose, the person who developed it, the date of the last update, the instructions for use (Article 13), and how the system interacts with hardware/software.
- **What's present:** The SOP defines clinical endpoints and intended purpose at a high level (Section 5.1), but there is **no procedure requiring documentation of**: the system's developer identity, version/date tracking in the technical documentation itself, a description of how the CDSS interacts with other systems (e.g., EHR, PACS), or the hardware/software environment specifications. The SOP references "Instructions for Use (IFU)" in Policy Statement 3 but never operationalizes IFU creation as a validation deliverable.
- **Gap:** The SOP lacks a structured template or procedure for producing the Annex IV §1 general description as a discrete, auditable technical documentation artifact.

### 2. Detailed Description of the System's Design and Development (Annex IV, §2)
- **What's required:** Design specifications, including algorithms, data requirements, design choices, and the development methodology.
- **What's present:** The SOP covers code freeze, containerization, and model weights (Section 5.4), but these are operational controls, not design documentation. There is **no requirement to document**: the rationale for algorithm selection, the model architecture, design assumptions, or the development methodology (e.g., supervised learning, ensemble methods).
- **Gap:** The SOP does not require documenting the design rationale, algorithm selection criteria, or architectural decisions — all required by Annex IV §2.

### 3. Description of Monitoring, Control, and Human Oversight (Annex IV, §3)
- **What's required:** Documentation of how the system is monitored during operation, including human oversight measures per Article 14.
- **What's present:** Section 7 covers post-market monitoring (PSI, override rates, bias metrics) and Section 8 covers escalation. However, there is **no documentation requirement for human oversight measures** as defined in Article 14 — specifically: (a) measures built into the system to enable oversight, (b) how deployers are enabled to understand capacities/limitations, remain aware of automation bias, correctly interpret outputs, override/ignore outputs, and intervene/stop the system (Article 14(4)(a)-(e)).
- **Gap:** The SOP is silent on documenting human oversight measures as required by Article 14 and Annex IV §3. The "stop button" or equivalent intervention mechanism (Article 14(4)(e)) is never mentioned.

### 4. Performance Metrics (Annex IV, §4)
- **What's present:** The SOP specifies sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, AUC, AUPRC, Brier score, calibration, and lead time (Section 5.5). This is strong.
- **What's partially missing:** Annex IV requires documentation of the **level of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity** (Article 15). The SOP covers accuracy metrics well but does **not require documentation of robustness testing** (resilience to errors, faults, inconsistencies, feedback loops — Article 15(3)) or **cybersecurity measures** (Article 15(4)). The SOP mentions container signing and environment segregation as technical controls (Section 6.1) but does not require cybersecurity testing or documentation of adversarial robustness.
- **Gap:** No requirement for robustness or cybersecurity testing documentation.

### 5. Risk Management System Documentation (Annex IV, §5)
- **What's required:** A detailed description of the risk management system per Article 9.
- **What's present:** The SOP references SOP-CLIN-002 (CDSS Risk Management and FMEA Procedure) and mentions FMEA in the acronyms. Section 8 covers exception handling and escalation. However, the SOP itself does **not require that the risk management system documentation be included in the technical documentation**, nor does it describe the continuous iterative risk management process required by Article 9(2) — identification, estimation, evaluation, and adoption of risk management measures throughout the lifecycle.
- **Gap:** The SOP defers entirely to SOP-CLIN-002 without ensuring the risk management documentation is integrated into the technical documentation package as required by Annex IV §5.

### 6. Description of Changes to the System (Annex IV, §6)
- **What's required:** Documentation of all changes made to the system through its lifecycle, including those predetermined at the time of initial conformity assessment.
- **What's present:** SOP-CLIN-005 (Clinical Algorithm Change Management and Versioning) is referenced. The SOP mentions code freeze and version tagging (Section 5.4). However, there is **no procedure requiring that changes be documented in the technical documentation** or that predetermined changes be declared at the time of conformity assessment (Article 13(3)(c)).
- **Gap:** No integration of change management documentation into the technical documentation package.

### 7. Applied Standards (Annex IV, §7)
- **What's required:** A list of harmonized standards applied, or where not applied in full, the means used to ensure compliance.
- **What's present:** Section 10.2 lists IEC 62304, ISO 13485, EU AI Act, EU MDR, NIST AI RMF, and HIPAA. However, the SOP does **not require a formal declaration of which standards were applied, which were partially applied, and what alternative means were used** for each relevant requirement.
- **Gap:** No procedure for producing the standards-conformance mapping required by Annex IV §7.

### 8. EU Declaration of Conformity (Annex IV, §8)
- **What's required:** The EU declaration of conformity per Article 47, containing the information in Annex V.
- **What's present:** Not mentioned anywhere in the SOP. The SOP references regulatory submission document control (SOP-REG-001) but does not address the EU declaration of conformity as a validation deliverable.
- **Gap:** **Completely missing.** The EU declaration of conformity is a mandatory Annex IV element and is not addressed.

### 9. Post-Market Monitoring Plan (Annex IV, §9)
- **What's required:** A detailed description of the post-market monitoring system and plan per Article 72, which must be part of the technical documentation.
- **What's present:** Section 7.1 requires a PMCF plan within 30 days of deployment and Section 7.2-7.3 covers KPIs and reporting cadence. However, the SOP does **not require that the post-market monitoring plan be included in the technical documentation** as required by Article 72(3) and Annex IV §9.
- **Gap:** The PMCF plan is treated as a separate post-deployment activity rather than an integral part of the technical documentation package.

---

## PART 2: GAPS AGAINST ARTICLE 17 — QUALITY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM

Article 17 requires a QMS documented in "a systematic and orderly manner in the form of written policies, procedures and instructions" covering 13 specific aspects. The SOP is one procedure within what is presumably a broader QMS, but it must itself address or integrate with these aspects. Here are the gaps:

### (a) Strategy for Regulatory Compliance, Including Conformity Assessment and Modification Management
- **What's present:** Section 1.1 references "applicable regulatory obligations." Section 4.6 references "EU AI Act requirements." The Regulatory Affairs Specialist role is Consulted.
- **What's missing:** There is **no documented strategy for regulatory compliance** — no reference to which conformity assessment procedure applies (Annex VI vs. Annex VII), no procedure for managing the conformity assessment process, and no integration with modification management procedures that would trigger re-assessment.
- **Gap:** The SOP lacks a regulatory compliance strategy and conformity assessment procedure.

### (b) Techniques, Procedures, and Systematic Actions for Design, Design Control, and Design Verification
- **What's present:** The SOP covers validation (Phase 3-4) but not design control or design verification. The scope is validation, not design.
- **What's missing:** There is **no design control procedure** — no design inputs, design outputs, design review, or design verification steps. The SOP assumes the CDSS already exists and needs validation.
- **Gap:** Design control and design verification are not addressed. The SOP only covers validation.

### (c) Techniques, Procedures, and Systematic Actions for Development, Quality Control, and Quality Assurance
- **What's present:** SOP-DS-001 (Model Development and Coding Standard) is referenced. The SOP covers QA through the Biostatistician's independent review.
- **What's missing:** The SOP does not integrate development QC/QA procedures into the validation workflow. There is no requirement that development QC/QA artifacts feed into the technical documentation.
- **Gap:** Partial — development QC/QA is referenced but not integrated.

### (d) Examination, Test, and Validation Procedures Before, During, and After Development, and Their Frequency
- **What's present:** The SOP covers pre-deployment validation (Phase 0-5) and post-deployment monitoring (Section 7). Annual re-validation is required (Section 7.3).
- **What's missing:** There is **no procedure for testing during development** (only pre-deployment validation). The SOP does not specify validation procedures for significant updates or changes.
- **Gap:** Testing during development is not addressed; change-triggered re-validation frequency is not specified.

### (e) Technical Specifications, Including Standards
- **What's present:** Section 10.2 lists external standards.
- **What's missing:** The SOP does **not require a gap analysis** when harmonized standards are not applied in full, nor does it specify the alternative means used to ensure compliance — as required by Article 17(1)(e).
- **Gap:** No procedure for documenting partial application of standards and alternative compliance means.

### (f) Systems and Procedures for Data Management
- **What's present:** Section 5.2 covers data curation, de-identification, hold-out set creation, and lockbox procedures. This is strong.
- **What's missing:** Article 17(1)(f) requires procedures for data acquisition, collection, analysis, labelling, storage, filtration, mining, aggregation, retention, and "any other operation." The SOP covers validation data but does **not address training data management** (data sourcing, labelling procedures, data quality assessment for training data).
- **Gap:** Training data governance procedures are not addressed; only validation/test data is covered.

### (g) Risk Management System (Article 9)
- **What's present:** SOP-CLIN-002 is referenced. FMEA is mentioned.
- **What's missing:** The SOP does not describe or require the continuous iterative risk management process mandated by Article 9(2), including: identification and analysis of known and reasonably foreseeable risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights; estimation and evaluation of risks under intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse; evaluation of risks from post-market monitoring data; and adoption of targeted risk management measures.
- **Gap:** The risk management system is deferred to another SOP without ensuring Article 9's specific requirements are met.

### (h) Post-Market Monitoring System (Article 72)
- **What's present:** Section 7 covers post-market monitoring comprehensively.
- **What's missing:** The SOP does not explicitly state that the post-market monitoring system must be **documented as part of the QMS** (Article 17(1)(h)) and that the plan must be part of the technical documentation (Article 72(3)).
- **Gap:** Minor — the system exists but its QMS integration is not formalized.

### (i) Procedures for Reporting Serious Incidents (Article 73)
- **What's present:** Section 8.2 mentions reporting to regulatory bodies and breach notification.
- **What's missing:** There is **no specific procedure for serious incident reporting** under Article 73 — no definition of what constitutes a "serious incident" under the AI Act, no timeline for reporting (15 days, or immediately for widespread infringement or death), no template, and no identification of the relevant market surveillance authority.
- **Gap:** The SOP lacks an Article 73-compliant serious incident reporting procedure.

### (j) Handling Communication with National Competent Authorities, Notified Bodies, etc.
- **What's present:** The Regulatory Affairs Specialist role is Consulted. Section 8.2 mentions reporting to regulatory bodies.
- **What's missing:** There is **no documented procedure for communication with national competent authorities, notified bodies, or other interested parties**. No contact points, no communication protocols, no language requirements.
- **Gap:** Communication handling procedures are not addressed.

### (k) Systems and Procedures for Record-Keeping
- **What's present:** The SOP references the Meridian Document Management System (MDMS), immudb artifact registry, S3 audit logs, and the Meridian Asset Registry.
- **What's missing:** There is **no explicit record retention period**. Article 50 requires documentation to be kept for **10 years** after the AI system is placed on the market. The SOP does not specify retention periods for validation artifacts, technical documentation, or QMS records.
- **Gap:** Record retention periods are not specified.

### (l) Resource Management, Including Security-of-Supply Measures
- **What's present:** Section 3 defines roles and responsibilities (RACI matrix). Section 6.2 covers vendor risk management (CRO SOC 2 Type II review, BAA).
- **What's missing:** There is **no resource management procedure** addressing security of supply — e.g., what happens if a critical third-party algorithm provider ceases operations, or if key personnel (e.g., the Biostatistician) are unavailable.
- **Gap:** Security-of-supply and resource continuity are not addressed.

### (m) Accountability Framework for Management and Staff
- **What's present:** Section 3 provides a RACI matrix. This is a good start.
- **What's missing:** Article 17(1)(m) requires an accountability framework "setting out the responsibilities of the management and other staff with regard to all the aspects listed in this paragraph." The RACI matrix covers validation roles but does **not extend to all 13 QMS aspects** — e.g., who is accountable for serious incident reporting, for communication with authorities, for record-keeping, for resource management.
- **Gap:** The accountability framework is incomplete — it covers validation but not the full QMS scope.

---

## SUMMARY OF CRITICAL GAPS

| # | Gap | Article/Annex | Severity |
|---|-----|---------------|----------|
| 1 | No EU Declaration of Conformity procedure | Annex IV §8 / Art. 47 | **Critical** |
| 2 | No serious incident reporting procedure (Art. 73) | Art. 17(1)(i) | **Critical** |
| 3 | No design control / design verification procedures | Art. 17(1)(b) | **Critical** |
| 4 | No regulatory compliance strategy / conformity assessment procedure | Art. 17(1)(a) | **Critical** |
| 5 | No human oversight documentation (Art. 14) | Annex IV §3 | **High** |
| 6 | No robustness or cybersecurity testing documentation | Annex IV §4 / Art. 15 | **High** |
| 7 | No communication handling procedure with authorities/notified bodies | Art. 17(1)(j) | **High** |
| 8 | No record retention period (10-year requirement) | Art. 17(1)(k) / Art. 50 | **High** |
| 9 | No standards-conformance gap analysis procedure | Art. 17(1)(e) / Annex IV §7 | **Medium** |
| 10 | No training data governance procedures | Art. 17(1)(f) | **Medium** |
| 11 | No resource management / security-of-supply procedure | Art. 17(1)(l) | **Medium** |
| 12 | Accountability framework incomplete (validation only, not full QMS) | Art. 17(1)(m) | **Medium** |
| 13 | Technical documentation not structured per Annex IV §1-§9 | Art. 11 / Annex IV | **Medium** |
| 14 | Risk management system deferred without Art. 9 integration assurance | Art. 17(1)(g) / Art. 9 | **Medium** |
| 15 | Post-market monitoring plan not integrated into technical documentation | Art. 72(3) / Annex IV §9 | **Low-Medium** |
| 16 | Change documentation not integrated into technical documentation | Annex IV §6 | **Low-Medium** |

---

## CONCLUSION

SOP-CLIN-001 is a **clinically robust validation SOP** with strong data integrity controls, statistical rigor, and post-market surveillance. However, it was designed primarily as a **clinical/technical validation procedure**, not as a comprehensive **regulatory technical documentation and QMS procedure** under the EU AI Act. The SOP assumes that other SOPs (CLIN-002, CLIN-005, REG-001, QA-005) fill the gaps, but it does not ensure integration of those procedures into a coherent technical documentation package (Annex IV) or a fully compliant QMS (Article 17).

The most critical gaps are structural: the SOP lacks procedures for the **EU Declaration of Conformity**, **Article 73 serious incident reporting**, **design control/verification**, and a **regulatory compliance strategy with conformity assessment**. These are not merely documentation omissions — they represent missing procedural infrastructure that a notified body would expect to see during an Annex VII conformity assessment (QMS assessment + technical documentation review).

**Compliance level: PARTIAL — significant gaps remain.**

---

**Compliance level: PARTIAL**
